chunk_summary: Guidelines for stabilizing children with severe wasting and complications during the first 48 hours in outpatient care while arranging referral, focusing on hydration, infection screening, safe feeding, and follow-up.
learning_objectives: Understand the triage process for children with severe wasting and complications.
Learn safe interim feeding practices for malnourished children during outpatient stabilization.
Identify clinical danger signs and assess appetite to determine referral urgency.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and community-based rehabilitation pathways.
key_facts: Children with severe wasting and complications require urgent facility-based care.
Appetite assessment is critical; poor appetite indicates need for inpatient care.
Rehydrate promptly if diarrhea with dehydration is present; severe dehydration necessitates admission.
Avoid thin, low-energy foods; prescribe small, frequent, energy-dense feeds if appetite is present.
Arrange follow-up within 48–72 hours and link to community systems like ANM/AWW.
Document anthropometric triggers to monitor progress and prevent default.
reference_quote: Children with severe wasting and complications (lethargy, persistent vomiting, hypothermia/fever, severe dehydration, respiratory distress, severe anemia, or inability to feed) require urgent facility-based care. — National Health Mission Operational Guidelines
document_key_topics: Triage of wasting and referral pathways
Safe feeding practices for malnourished children
